Uh we've got I asked AI Jeremy I said yo what are you talking to all the inner circle members about like what do you think amongst literally all of them you know would be a great topic for this week's group call and I also asked it I was like what do you want to know more about and it gave us the answer of how to build and optimize high converting upsell and cross-sell strategies.
[00:30] Uh we're going to be going through structuring upsells that feel like a no-brainer how to frame cross sales without overwhelming people using urgency and scarcity to drive the conversions post purchase. And then I'm going to give you obviously a bunch of real world examples of uh these things in action. So should be a banger of a call.
[01:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How to Build and Optimize High Converting Upsell and Cross-Sell Strategies"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Título principal do tópico da call, layout limpo e profissional
[01:15] Um crazy territory that we're in here with AI Jeremy coming up with our weekly group calls now. But again, it's all based on what you guys are talking to it about. So should be an exciting one. Um without further ado, let's dive in and get started here.
[01:30] Okay, so for starters, like I said, group topic is how to build and optimize high converting upsell and cross-sell strategies. Let's just get started with understanding the differences here so we're on the same page from a vocabulary perspective.
[01:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Upsell vs Cross-Sell"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação entre dois conceitos, layout dividido mostrando diferenças
[02:00] Okay, so an upsell is typically something that happens post purchase. Okay, it could have been something low ticket, could have been something high ticket. An upsell is simply something that they purchase after.
[02:15] Okay, a cross-sell is typically when the person is there to buy a specific thing and you sell them something different instead. So, as an example, maybe somebody shows up to buy a filet mignon at a restaurant, you cross-sell them into another cut of steak instead.
[02:30] Okay? It could be more expensive, could be less expensive. It's not necessarily about getting more money out of the person when you cross-sell. It's about selling them what's most appropriate for them or and/or potentially bundling a purchase with something else as well at the time of purchase.
[02:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Three-Part Framework"
NÚMEROS: 3 categorias
VISUAL: Três colunas ou seções distintas, organização estruturada em framework
[03:00] I think of all this in a very simple way. Okay, I structure it around a three-part framework. I think everybody should follow this same strategy. You have do-it-yourself, done with you, and done for you.
[03:15] Okay, I think right away this makes it extremely natural. And it just categorizes things in a way that make a lot of sense when you compartmentalize all of your all of the offers that you have into these three separate categories.
[03:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Do-It-Yourself Offers"
NÚMEROS: $5,000 (preço dos cursos)
VISUAL: Primeira categoria do framework, exemplos de produtos DIY, ícone de aprendizado autodidata
[03:45] Okay? I always use my offers as an example for this because I think they work great and we think with this regularly. So, let me show you what we do. Okay, first of all, the do-it-yourself offers that we have. These are all of our courses.
[04:00] Okay, for us, that's our master internet marketing offer as an example. We also have our high ticket agency offer. Again, these are both just courses, okay? Very simple. They're both just courses. Both of these, regardless of which one we're referring to, is $5,000.
[04:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Done With You"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados neste momento
VISUAL: Segunda categoria do framework, ícone de colaboração ou parceria
[04:45] Okay, very simple. Uh, we have the done with you. Done with you would be what you're in right now as an example. So, we have the inner circle and in addition to that, we have our consulting deal. We will soon have a standalone version of Jeremy AI as well.
[05:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Done For You"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados neste momento
VISUAL: Terceira categoria do framework, ícone de serviço completo ou hands-on
[05:15] Okay. Done on done for you for us is our agency services. Okay. Now, without going through price yet, just to focus on the offers themselves. Okay. You can add as many offers to these three categories that you want, but they should always fit into one of these three categories.
[05:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Determines the Right Offer?"
NÚMEROS: 3 variáveis
VISUAL: Lista de três fatores determinantes, layout de checklist ou bullet points
[06:00] Okay, because it's based on what level of help the person wants and how much involvement they want from you in your organization and or how much they want to spend that can determine which one of these three is most appropriate for them.
[06:15] Okay, I'm going to go through that again. I'm going to write it down though. So what determines this is price, level of involvement, and outcome speed.
[06:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Three Decision Variables"
NÚMEROS: 3 fatores
VISUAL: Três elementos destacados: "PRICE", "LEVEL OF INVOLVEMENT", "OUTCOME SPEED" em destaque visual
[06:45] Okay? So we take these three variables and we help determine what's going to be best for the person. Okay? Somebody might come to us, and this happens to us all the time. They come to us for agency services and they make like shit all money and they would just be an awful deal.
[07:00] And we would never work with them and they're like, "Hey, I want to be an agency client." And they originate through our agency application as an example. It's like, okay, I mean that that's fine. Just because you want that doesn't mean that that's most appropriate for you.
[07:15] We are going to move you into what makes the most sense. That'd be a cross-sell example. Just because that person wants that doesn't mean we want to sell it to them. Okay? or vice versa. You might want to sell something to somebody, but they don't necessarily think that that's best for them.
[07:30] Okay? So, again, we want to have other opportunities to cross-sell the person into instead of just losing money. That's why we have these three different categories.
[08:00] For us, we commonly, and some of you on this very call might be in this case where you've wanted to work with us as an agency and honestly, we were either at capacity. You didn't have the shit that you needed built out. Uh maybe you just didn't have the right offer for us and we didn't think that it was going to be something that we could scale to the couple million bucks a month we needed to scale to.
[08:30] You know, whatever it was, we determined, hey, it's likely better for you to be in the inner circle. Or, hey, we're still happy to help, but we don't want to do it hands-on. We think it'd make more sense to do a consulting deal.

NÚMEROS: $4,000/mês → $15,000/mês (diferença de $11,000), $300K/mês → $600K/mês (crescimento de receita)
VISUAL: Caso real de upsell, crescimento de receita do cliente, house money concept
[27:00] I'm going to give you guys a really good one. So, just recently, we had two inner circle members. They've made enough money from the inner circle where upselling into us helping them as an agency was pretty much free.
[27:15] They made enough money in the inner circle and were able to pocket enough money to where our cost as an agency was literally free. They were just using house money to play for it at that point.
[27:30] And my intention, this is very important to understand, my intention with the inner circle is not to take every one of you and upsell you into agency services.
[27:45] you know, I'm happy with you exactly where you're at, but when it becomes appropriate for you to upsell to agency services and you bring it up to me, I'll happily take you up on that opportunity if it makes sense for both of us to do.
[28:00] And what am I going to do to help you do that? I'm going to make you a fuckload of money. So, you're pretty much playing with house money when it comes time to make that decision.
[28:30] And I'm going make it easy for the for the most recent person that I just cross-sold like that. I did. You could technically call that an upsell, I guess. Going from the inner circle to agency services, right?
[28:45] In that case, that person was paying $4,000 a month. Now, they pay $15,000 a month. So, there was $11,000 a month in difference in cost. Okay? Not that much money.
[29:00] And guess what? I you guys want to take a guess? They went from $300,000 a month when they joined in to over $600,000 a month with where they're at now. So, there's a difference there of $300,000 a month.
[29:15] How easy is it to afford an extra $11,000 a month when that's the case? Okay. And in terms of ease, it's really easy, man. I send you an email that says final follow-up actions. It's just got a list of shit I need you to send to me.
[29:30] Like, hey, give me access to this. Send me these login. You know, film some ads like this. It's pretty simple. Might take you at most like an hour or two to onboard us.
[29:45] So, it's an $11,000 difference out of an extra $300,000 a month that the person made while being a part of the inner circle. And it was super easy to onboard. You see what I'm saying?

[32:00] All I do is just email David and I just say like, "Hey, give me a quote on this vehicle. I'm trading in this vehicle. assume I get this much for the vehicle. Within a day, he sends me an email that says, "Here's how much it's going to cost out of pocket, Mr. Haynes. Here's how much it is monthly."
[32:30] And I just say, "Sweet." And then I call up the dealership. I talk to the financing people before I talk to the dealership. I call up the dealership and I'm like, "Hey, you got this car right here. I'm already approved on funding and uh I'm ready to rock and roll.
[32:45] I just need you to be at this on my trade-in and if you can be at that on my trade in, I'll do the deal literally right now." And then they typically will get my VIN on the trade-in, some photos of the trade-in, confirm that they could do that price, and then that's it.
[33:00] I get a tow truck, typically sent by the dealer. That's under the ease category. And from there, really simple, man. I just wire somebody money, sign a few papers, and I get a car that shows up at my house.

[35:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Four Seasons Hotel - Bad Example"
NÚMEROS: 11:00 PM (horário da call), 20+ minutos (duração da call), 30 minutos (tempo de espera)
VISUAL: Exemplo negativo de dificuldade, falta de ease, processo complicado de upgrade
[36:00] Okay, here's here's a great example. I was at the Four Seasons in Chicago and I needed a uh I needed a two-bedroom suite. I had one of my staff members that was going to show up and it was my video guy and it would have just been a hell of a lot easier to have him like in the unit there cuz we were going to do a lot of filming.
[36:30] And long story short, I was in a one-bedroom suite at the time. I called down to the front desk and I'm like, "Hey, is it possible for me to upgrade to a two-bedroom suite? I'm happy to spend the extra money. Just tell me what the price is. Tell me if it's available and let's do it."
[36:45] Okay. Right away, this became like almost like a challenge to the person at the front desk. It was as though this just doesn't happen that often, and they had to talk to like two different people.
[37:00] So, initially, it just came off as like, "Hey, Mr. Haynes, like, you know, let me let me call some people real quick and see if I can do that. um just give me a few moments and I'll give you a call back.
[37:15] So it takes like I don't know 30-ish minutes. I get a call back from the front desk and they just made it sound like it was going to be such a pain in the ass.
[37:30] Like they had the unit available. They had my card on file. Literally all they'd have to do is just upgrade me. But for some reason they wanted me to wait until 11:00 a.m. the next day.
[37:45] Like they had the room available literally to go into in that exact moment that night. But they wanted me to wait until 11:00 a.m. the next day. Something about the system and like all these random things that I didn't give a shit about.
[38:00] And I'm like, "Yeah, but I got my video guy here like literally right now. Like he just landed. I like Are you going to make me buy another room to put the guy into? And then tomorrow you're going to transition both of us into the two-bedroom suite?"
[38:15] And they started again that that complicated it even further in these people's eyes. and they started going through all this shit This ended up being like a fucking 20 minute phone call for the second call and we could not conclude the call with a simple, "Yeah, Mr. Haynes, let me go ahead and charge your card right now.
[38:45] You're going to be going up to unit blank. Um, I'll have somebody come to the unit. We'll have a bell guy come, get all your luggage together, take it up to the unit, we'll hand you the cards, you know, you'll be good to go."
[39:00] Uh, it could have been that easy, but instead it took like a fucking 20 minute phone call. And I ended the 20-minute second phone call literally just like, I'm not even gonna I'm not even going to put this guy in this hotel.
[39:15] I'm going to get him a different hotel somewhere else that's nearby. And that took me like two minutes to do, you know? So, the Four Seasons didn't get that extra money out of me.
[39:30] And honestly, they kind of rubbed me the wrong way. It gave me like a feeling that they're incompetent, you know, that they're stupid or something, you You know, it felt like it should have just been something so easy and simple.

[1:16:00] So, first of all, when you go to book anything at Alenia and you try to get a reservation, first of all, good luck. Okay, you're probably going to be booked pretty far out.
[1:16:15] So, you're going to go to book a table here. It's going to be through this thing called talk. You're going to try to find a table, okay? There's not going to be anything.
[1:16:30] You're going to go like a fucking month out and then look at that, right? You got dinner options. You don't want to go in this salon. The salon's upstairs. You want to go to the gallery.
[1:16:45] And as you can see, there's not fucking available in the gallery. No matter what day you look, everything's fucking sold out. So, you're going to push into the next month. Maybe you're looking at May now, right?
[1:17:00] And as you can see, there's still not fucking shit available in the gallery, which is the place I was just telling you you actually want to eat.

[1:27:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Same Product, Different Terms"
NÚMEROS: 3 anos (McLaren warranty), 4 anos (Rolls-Royce warranty)
VISUAL: Exemplo de venda do mesmo produto com termos diferentes: financiamento, warranty, estrutura de pagamento
[1:28:00] We can also, as you've noticed in these examples I provided to you, I'm not buying a different McLaren when I go and buy the 2024 versus the 2025. I'm buying the same fucking car, just a different way of buying it. The difference was the funding.
[1:28:15] The difference for you might be out of pocket financing, which financing partner you're going to use when somebody buys your stuff.
[1:28:30] That additional thing I want to point out with the McLaren is you get three years of a new car warranty with McLaren. McLarens are p any British car is a piece of shit and you always want to make sure that you buy it with a new car warranty or a warranty at the least that you can extend.
[1:28:45] You know why British cars depreciate like absolute fucking rocks when they go out of war. It's just they go out of warranty. That's all it is.
[1:29:00] You see a fucking Rolls-Royce. Rolls-Royces have a four-year new car warranty. So, if you buy a 2020 Rolls-Royce right now, you'll see that they're cheap as fuck Why? They're out of warranty.
[1:29:15] It's an illusion. You never buy a Rolls-Royce out of warranty. You never buy an Aston Martin out of warranty. You never buy a McLaren out of warranty. You always buy these cars under a warranty.
[1:29:30] That's why they're so expensive when you buy them, because they're going to be fixing the shit out of them all the time on their dime, not your dime. That's why they become cheap as fuck after they're out of warranty. It's on your dime, not their dime, that they fix the thing afterwards.
[1:29:45] That difference made a lot of sense to me. It's the same exact product, but the difference in that case is one extra year of warranty. Arguably, really necessary.
[1:30:00] Sometimes you could sell people the exact same thing, but you can upsell them to a slightly better version of it based on the conditions of the deal, like an extra year of warranty.

[1:41:30] We're like, "Listen, honestly, just based on the speed and for us to like increase the probability of you hitting that speed, we think consulting would make a lot more sense because we'd have more involvement. More involvement would lead to faster results.
[1:41:45] You see, that's not us trying to push something on them in that case just to make more money. Quite the opposite. It's us trying to push them to something that can meet their goals and outcomes they have for desires, right?
[1:42:00] It's not just us trying to make more cash. It's us trying to help them get their result.

[1:43:00] Here's what the differences would be. Here's why I think it would make sense. Here's why I think it wouldn't make sense. And I don't press him to do it. I just tell him, "Here's what I think makes sense."
[1:43:15] And ultimately, if he comes back to me and says, "Yeah, you know, I I think I would like to do that." As you heard, it would be based on the fact he's got a fuckload more closers.
[1:43:30] and he wants to fill them up right away, which he still doesn't need to upsell to the the the agency services to accomplish. He can do that just fine on his own.
[1:43:45] The main reasons he transition is is he's let down by his current team. He doesn't think his he doesn't believe in his current team. He doesn't like the standards of his current team. He wants to buy speed and he wants to buy standards.
[1:44:00] That would be the main thing that would make sense for for the reason he would transition from one to the other. You see, and sometimes people don't know that until they're in the deal already, right?
[1:44:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Garrett White Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $22M/ano (receita de Garrett), 3 horas (em 8 horas de consulting), 2 equipes de marketing presentes
VISUAL: Caso de consulting que virou agency, descoberta de gap de talento durante consulting session
[1:44:45] When we we get instances where we do consulting deals where the person's like, "Yeah, you know, like I already got a great team." We come in and we start consulting literally after the end of the first call.
[1:45:00] I I'll give you a perfect example. It's a guy, probably one of my favorite clients I've ever worked with. I love this guy named Garrett White. Garrett does about 22 million gear. Okay.
[1:45:15] Garrett is a uh mentor to married businessmen with kids. Okay. To help them master those three different areas to master the family life, the relationship, and their business. Garrett makes people a fuckload of money. He's a really good guy.
[1:45:30] Now, to be clear, Garrett, when we initially started working with him, was a consulting deal, and we did an in-person consulting deal nonetheless. I flew out from Miami to Dana Point, California, met with Garrett and his team.
[1:45:45] He had a he had two different marketing teams in person. A guy named Stephen and a guy named Runar who were there to learn from me some of my content ad strategies. We were specifically going to teach him the harvester, which is a big mass market content ad strategy.
[1:46:00] Now, I get about three hours into an 8-hour consulting day with Garrett. Garrett pulls me outside and says, "I'm going to go back in there and fire my current team. I want I want to hire you to do this instead."
[1:46:15] Just makes way more sense for you to do it. Honestly, after hearing you talk, seeing how you are, there are night and day differences between you and my current team. I' I'd much rather have you do it in comparison to them.
[1:46:30] And I was very uncomfortable. I was like I was like I was like, Garrett, you know, no offense. I was like, it's kind of fucked up, you know. He was like he was like, don't worry, I'm going to take care of those guys.
[1:46:45] He explained like the whole plan to me of how he was going to transition them to like other things. They both ended up not getting fired to be clear. He more so meant he was going to take him off that current responsibility they had and have him do other stuff in the business instead.
[1:47:00] He ended up making the Steven guy the CEO of his warrior greens business which like a supplement brand and he ended up taking Runar Runar into more of like a like a tactical marketing role like building funnels and emails and marketing automation and stuff like that which Garrett does a fucking lot of.
[1:47:15] So that was a necessary position and he ended up hiring us full-time as an advertiser. Okay. Point is, that sometimes happens where we do a consulting deal and then the client just sees there's just a substantial fucking difference in the standards of their team and how we are versus how they are, a talent gap, if you will, and chooses to work with us instead.
[1:47:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Upsell for Outcome, Not Money"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Filosofia ética de upsell: fazer pelo benefício do cliente, não pelo próprio ganho financeiro
[1:48:00] Okay? Again, just a natural upsell that occurs there. Okay? And also, I just want to be clear. If we walk away from a consulting deal and we believe, and again, it's very important to understand.
[1:48:15] I want to make this abundantly clear. Most of you will attempt to upsell and cross-sell somebody solely for your own financial benefit.
[1:48:30] I I know this sounds a little fucking I'm not trying to I'm not trying to like virtue signal or anything. I don't do that, okay? I upsell for the benefit of of outcome to the person.
[1:48:45] If I talk to your team in a consulting deal and I genuinely don't believe in them, if I don't believe in you, I will drop you and I will tell you I don't I don't believe in your staff. I don't believe in you. I don't believe in the outcome of what I've sold you on.
[1:49:00] I'm not going to do the deal with you in this way. And if that leads to me upselling you to an agency deal where I can then do it to help you get the outcome, it is what it is. I'm not doing it in that case just to make the extra money. I'm doing it because I legitimately don't believe in your team.
[1:49:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Roy Case Study - Downsell Example"
NÚMEROS: $6K (custo inner circle para Roy + parceiro), 3 meses (tempo sem resultado), 1 semana (tentativa de educação)
VISUAL: Exemplo de downsell por falta de competência, "Does a pixel go on the website?" como red flag
[1:49:45] In that example, we had an inner circle member, his name was Roy. Roy had a marketer that he had with him named Jerome. I believed in Jerome. Roy was a very hands-off agency owner.
[1:50:00] And listen to this. He was an agency owner. He's in the contracting niche. So Roy and Jerome are within their first month of being a part of the inner circle.
[1:50:15] And Roy hits me up and says, "Hey, I want to drop Jerome. It's been like three months. I haven't really gotten that much result from the guy yet.
[1:50:30] And uh I'm like, Roy, I think that's an awful idea. No offense to you. I don't think you know jack fucking shit about marketing. And uh I also don't think based on your behavior of being in the inner circle so far that you're going to really take on the initiative of learning.
[1:50:45] The entire inner circle is about learning, dude. It's about consumption. If you fail to consume the information and then do stuff with it, you're not going to get a result.
[1:51:00] And I take the people a part of this group extremely seriously. You on the call right now and being a part of the group are the product. If you go out there and fail to make more money after being a part of this group, you make me look like shit You make me look like doodoo.
[1:51:15] Okay? You reflect my skill set to make people more money. And if you come in here and make more money and you fail to do that, how does that reflect on me? Makes me look awful.
[1:51:30] So I tell Roy, I say, Roy, I don't believe in you at all, dude. I believed in you because of Jerome. And if you want to drop Jerome, let me just set the expectation. You better put put on your student cap and go through a fuckload of material and then turn around and use it.
[1:51:45] Cuz no offense, I'm sitting here looking you up right now in your training library and you haven't even logged in yet, dude. Like, how do you expect me to to sell you ethically, you know?
[1:52:00] So, anyway, long story short, Roy's like, "Don't worry, Jeremy." Like, I take what you just said very seriously, too, and I'll fucking do it. Like, don't don't worry.
[1:52:15] So, Roy goes for about a week, starts the education process. He is consuming a fuckload of information. He starts going through the master internet marketing classes, weekly group calls, asking questions. Good, good trend.
[1:52:30] And then out of nowhere, Roy hits me up and he asks the dumbest fucking question I've ever gotten from an agency owner who's in my inner circle program. Roy asks, "Does a pixel go on the website?"
[1:52:45] Now listen, I'm here to help you, okay? I don't want to make it sound like you can't ask me questions. You can ask me questions all you want. That's what I'm here to help you with. Here to help you make more money and answer your question, get you the information you need.
[1:53:00] But when you ask me some shit like that, I lost all belief in Roy in a matter of five words. Okay? lost all belief. He rug-pulled himself of all belief I had to the guy.
[1:53:15] I immediately dropped him out of the inner circle and I said, "Roy, you spent 6K for you and your business partner to be here. We're just going to chalk it up that you got one month of the inner circle at no cost and I'm just going to keep you in the master internet marketing group."
[1:53:30] All right? I think that's much more appropriate for you. Has all the information that you need. I'm going to keep you there. I'm going to stop your billing. I'm going to take you out of the inner circle immediately.
[1:53:45] Okay? I can't have people like you in there. You're gonna turn off all the people who are in there right now if you ask that type of question in the group.
[1:54:00] I'm not going to have you there interacting with people at a mastermind. I give a deep shit about who all of the members are amongst when they're here and who they have next to them.
[1:54:15] I want them to have certainty in those people that they're smart and can help them, too. I don't I I just simply put I lost belief in you in that one question.
[1:54:30] Not trying to say that it's a question that's not something you need to know the answer to. I'm just saying this offer is not for you.
[1:54:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ethical Upsell/Downsell Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Mover pessoas entre ofertas baseado no que é genuinamente melhor para elas, não apenas para ganho próprio
[1:55:00] I downsold Roy into a lifetime product that won't charge him again because I felt that was more appropriate for him.
[1:55:15] I want to be clear when I say this, okay? I tell you that story for a very simple reason. I think it is most appropriate for you to move people between offers based on what is genuinely best for them.
[1:55:30] that also aligns to their outcomes and expectations. I do not encourage you to just move people between products for the sake of money and your own financial benefit.
[1:55:45] I think that it is far better if you do it from the perspective I just shared with you. Do it based on what is best for the person. And these become seamless transitions to take somebody from one to the other.
[1:56:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Edward Case Study - Checking In"
NÚMEROS: 1 ano (pagamento anual), 4 meses (tempo decorrido sem ação aparente)
VISUAL: Exemplo de check-in proativo, verificação de progresso, oportunidade de upsell baseada em falta de resultado
[1:56:30] I'll give you a great example. I'll use Edward as a perfect one. Edward had a marketer show up at the first mastermind. I think it's his marketer. I'm honestly not sure because I haven't heard from the guy once. I only met him in person at a mastermind.
[1:56:45] Edward joined the inner circle for a year. He paid annually. His goal is obviously to make more money. He wants to learn how to advertise. Edward's here with us on this call right now learning how to do these kinds of things.
[1:57:00] Okay. But you know what? I haven't heard from Edward or his marketer. I haven't heard from any result they've gotten yet. I haven't heard I don't even know if Edward's spending money on paid ads right now. I haven't heard of it. I haven't got It's
[1:57:15] And it's weird that he wants to learn how to do paid ads, but he hasn't asked me a single question about paid ads, which almost all of you will obviously as you start paid advertising and scaling.
[1:57:30] It makes me want to hit up Edward and say, ' Edward, what's going on? Are you doing what you what you wanted to do? Are you getting the outcome that you wanted to get?
[1:57:45] And if Edward said, you know, Jeremy, I'm actually not really doing paid advertising. I would say, well, Edward, it's been almost four months. I mean, we're through the whole first quarter. So, you're telling me you didn't take a single action? Why not?
[1:58:00] And if Edward said something like, "Well, I don't know, man. I mean, my my advertiser just is overwhelmed or something. I'm I'm not sure." you know, whatever the reasons are.
[1:58:15] That would be an opportunity where I would be like, "Well, Edward, I think it makes more sense then for us to either get more involved to hold your people more accountable to doing what the fuck you're here to do or just letting us take it over."
[1:58:30] You see? So, it's like that's not me hitting up Edward saying, "Hey, Edward, I want to upsell you into agency services." That's me hitting up Edward saying, "Hey, man. You hired us for an outcome. Are you getting the outcome?
[1:58:45] And if not, and you're moving like a fucking turtle, maybe it makes sense to spend a little more money to get what you're after and get the outcome.
[1:59:00] You see, like I believe in what Edward sells. I fucking hate taxes. I spend so much in taxes. It'd be so easy for me to tap into my own pain to tell Edward the best hooks to use, what to film, and obviously just run the ads.
[1:59:15] I'd be great at targeting his ideal demographic. But again, it's like the most cost-effective thing for Edward to do is to just be a part of the inner circle. And as long as he actually uses it to get the outcome that he's after, we're happy keeping him there.
[1:59:30] We don't care if he upsells or not. But if he hires us and does not get the outcome or is not on pace to get the outcome he's after, makes me feel unethical, makes me feel bad.
[1:59:45] It makes me feel obligated to hit him up and say, "I think it makes more sense to do this instead." You know, and it's not, this is the main thing I want you to understand for why I use this as an example.
[2:00:00] It's not from a place of just trying to get more money out of Edward. It is from a place of trying to get Edward the outcome since his current team is failing him to get the outcome. If that actually is the case, I just use it as an example because I haven't heard from Edward.
[2:00:15] You see what I'm saying? That's what you guys got to do with the people that are currently spending money with you. It's that simple. It's not rocket science.
[2:00:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "No Static Timeline for Upsells"
NÚMEROS: 7 semanas (exemplo de timeline arbitrária - NÃO fazer isso)
VISUAL: Crítica a email sequences estáticas, não existe duração fixa para upsell
[2:01:00] There's And notice how I haven't said, "All right, guys. So, after somebody gives you money, you're going to wait seven weeks, and then you're going to use this specific email sequence, and you're going to try to upsell them into the other thing.
[2:01:15] Why am I not telling you that? Think about that. This doesn't make sense. It's like there's not a static duration of time to just tell you like, "Oh, dude, everybody upsells after this."
[2:01:30] You know, it's based on whether they achieve the outcome, whether they are failing to achieve the outcome, whether they are unsettled with how slow it is taking them to get the outcome they hired you to get it.
[2:01:45] Maybe they became flushed with cash and they've wanted a faster outcome the entire time and now they're in a better financial position. Maybe something changed.
[2:02:00] Like I wouldn't take on Andrew when he's at 100K a month and can't fucking hire a salesperson. Andrew at 200k a month after he's taken on salespeople and is no longer on the phones. Him hiring the next set of closers gives me the confidence to take them on.
[2:02:15] You see? So, it's it's again conditions conditions of the deal. Okay? All these things are what determine when and how you upsell and cross-sell. That's it.
[2:02:30] It's not a static answer. It's not some fucking secret email sequence. There's not a finite amount of time that goes by where you just have your sales people.

[2:29:00] Timothy: Yeah. I think that the key is also just selling something where like know if you're going to sell somebody something, selling it for high enough of a price where you like where it's worth your time to answer their questions.
[2:29:15] Exactly right, man. Yeah. We don't sell Master Internet Marketing for 5K for no fucking reason, you know? It's for an incentive that I'll go in there and answer people's questions when they have it.
[2:29:30] I'll make things like Jeremy AI available to them in very varied forms. You know, it's like again, just like you guys here, it's like if you've been and and again, I know you're relatively new comparatively to some of the people that have been in here for like years,
[2:29:45] but you'll notice through time like you'll be quite impressed with the very consistent and constant improvement of this community and group. It's it's evolving constantly in order to continue helping you make more money.
[2:30:00] And why? Well, it's because you pay me enough money for me to give a shit about doing that. So, if you're undercharging yourself, I mean, that in itself is your problem, you know?
[2:30:15] Timothy: Yeah.
[2:30:20] Incentivize yourself to give a shit you know? Otherwise, you just kind of sound like you're bitching.
[2:30:30] I don't ever I don't ever sound like how you're sound, you know? I don't ever make it seem like, oh, it's a pain in the ass to like help people. It's like, no, dude, I fucking love when you guys continue to ask stuff.
[2:30:45] Your your feedback consistently helps me in many different ways. helps me think of next YouTube videos to create. It helps me consider what training material I currently lack. You know, it helps me understand what I could do to build and support you further.
[2:31:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Financial Modeling Tool Preview"
NÚMEROS: Domingo (data de lançamento planejada)
VISUAL: Preview de nova ferramenta de modelagem financeira sendo desenvolvida, exemplo de melhoria contínua baseada em feedback

[2:31:45] It's like this is um let me see if I can find it real quick for you. Yeah, it's pretty sick. It uh should be rolled out ideally by Sunday depending on if we have it done or not.
[2:32:00] So, you have all these different funnel variations here that uh you know, and like I said, there's some things we got to fix. Like I can't scroll down in this fucking thing right now to see the rest of them.
[2:32:15] Long story short, like you have the calculator here, and you have all these different variables that you can throw into it. You have the conservative, ambitious, and underperforming model. And then it shows the differences between the three.
[2:32:30] You have comparison mode to be able to determine the three different outcomes between them. You know, you have different ways to be able to view the data.
[2:32:45] This is an attempt on my end to improve beyond just sharing you guys a spreadsheet on Google Sheets, you know, to help give you a more like visual version of the software that is a little better and easier to use. Does that make sense?
[2:33:00] Timothy: 100%.

VISUAL: Ciclo de feedback: clientes dizem que são ruins em X → você cria ferramenta para ajudar com X
[2:33:30] Yeah. But again, it's like you guys consistently telling me that you suck at financial modeling shows me that I need to help you more with financial modeling. So, what do I do?
[2:33:45] But if we were if we were paying you 97 bucks a month, you wouldn't give a shit
[2:34:00] Yeah, I wouldn't give a single shit 97. I don't even know what I could I think I spend more on like food every time I eat food than $97. I don't know what maybe toothpaste and stuff, I guess. I don't know. Soap, you know,
[2:34:15] you got to pay enough to like genuinely give a shit And that number changes, you know what I mean? Like the OG members from the inner circle from like 2019 pay way less than what you pay now.
[2:34:30] But that's because the level of involvement and outcome that you guys have now. And also just the fact it's a scarce resource. So price goes up through time determines, you know, just okay, I've gotten a lot richer.
[2:34:45] In order for me to continue sustaining, giving a deep shit about your guys' outcomes, I need people to pay more money, you know, and look at it now. It's like that worked out great for all of you. You know what I mean?
[2:35:00] Timothy: Yeah. 100%.
[2:35:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Collective Unit vs Individual"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de que valor vem do coletivo, não apenas de cada indivíduo isolado
[2:35:30] And it's not also, it's important to know, Timothy, it's not just you individually in this case that makes me give a shit you all collectively as a unit pay enough for me to give a deep shit about this whole thing at a pretty significant level.
[2:35:45] You know what I mean? That's also important to consider. It's it's not always just made up of the individuals. It's a collective unit. They won't look at themselves that way, but that's how you'll look at it. Does that make sense?
[2:36:00] Timothy: Yeah, 100%. 100%.
[2:36:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Question About Ad Account Support"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[2:36:45] Jeremy, one one quick question. Um, so I've been providing basically access to my rep where I've been helping like brands in my coaching program get their ad accounts unbanned, like ads approved, just like stuff that I've never originally told people they would be getting inside my program.
[2:37:00] And so my question is, should I just include that and like increase the price or like add that more as like an upsell? like, hey, um, you know, upsell when I'm closing them.
[2:37:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Personal Choice on Service Inclusion"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Resposta: depende da preferência pessoal, pode incluir no preço ou fazer referral sem comissão
[2:37:45] That's up to you. Um, as as an example, like sometimes you guys ask for stuff like that. It's like I don't include that and what you guys pay. We just refer you to people that can do that kind of.
[2:38:00] We don't even make money when you when we refer you to those different people, you know? We just try to find like, you know, vetted contacts that can help and plugs for things like that as those things come up.
[2:38:15] But that's up to you. I mean, again, it's a very like personal question, I guess, for how you choose to go about doing it. Um, if you feel like it happens so consistently that it makes sense to just bake that into the price, kind of like the wine pairing option.
[2:38:30] It can also be like a like a bundle offer, you know. Um, I would I wouldn't find a lot of value in myself getting involved in that. I think it'd be too little money. It's transactional and I don't know, it just kind of complicates my life beyond what I would want to do.
[2:38:45] So, I wouldn't do it, but it's up to you. You know what I mean? You just got to weigh that against those same things I was just thinking of. Is it worth the money? Is it worth the time and effort? Is it worth the responsibility of helping people get those outcomes?
[2:39:00] You know, answer is yes, then yeah, makes sense.
[2:39:15] Cool.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$5,000 - Preço dos cursos DIY (Master Internet Marketing, High Ticket Agency)
$5,000/mês - Inner Circle (variável, OGs pagam menos)
$20,000/mês - Consulting (weekly 1-hour calls)
$15,000/mês - Agency Services (exemplo de upsell do inner circle)
$4,000/mês → $15,000/mês - Diferença de $11,000/mês em caso de upsell
$300K/mês → $600K/mês - Crescimento de receita de cliente que depois fez upsell
$100K-$110K/mês → $200K/mês - Crescimento de Andrew no inner circle
$1.6M/mês - Cliente estagnado por 2 anos que foi cross-sold para consulting
$22M/ano - Receita de Garrett White (caso de consulting → agency)
$460,000 - McLaren 750S Spider
$105K - Out-of-pocket para McLaren
$400K+ - Preço base McLaren 750S
$250,000 - Diferença de preço entre 911 e Ferrari 812 GTS
$60K - Out-of-pocket para 911 Turbo S
$200K+ - Out-of-pocket para Ferrari 812 GTS
$25,000/mês - Penthouse rent
$6,550/mês - Segunda casa rent
$31,550/mês - Total rent combinado
$300,000+ - Richard Mille Tourbillon
~$100,000 - Richard Mille low-cost (comprado por Brez)
$50,000 - Base package de Edward (tax services)
$250,000 - Top package de Edward ($200K diferença)
$375/pessoa - Base price Alinea
$295 - Wine pairing regular (Alinea)
$795 - Wine pairing premium (Alinea)
15 cursos - Menu Jungsik
17 cursos - Com supplements Jungsik
~1,000 vídeos - Biblioteca de cursos total
900+ vídeos - Vídeos de curso disponíveis
2,000 mensagens/mês - Limite Jeremy AI para curso DIY
1M mensagens/mês - Plano pago Jeremy AI (Jeremy)
15 mensagens - Total para fechar negócio 911 com Chicago Motorcars
4 horas - Tempo para fechar McLaren com Prestige Imports
3 dias - Tempo de espera resposta McLaren Bank/Chase
$150K - Out-of-pocket real McLaren Bank (vs $100K prometido)
$5,800/mês - LLP monthly
$3,600/mês - Chase monthly
3 carros - Comprados em 2025 (Jan, Fev, Mar)
3 anos - McLaren warranty
4 anos - Rolls-Royce warranty
3 horas - Em 8 horas consulting com Garrett antes de pedir agency
7 Paganis - Na The Collection ($2M-$7M cada)
<12 pessoas - Capacidade Alinea por seating
5 minutos - Distância de The Collection de Jeremy
3 estados - Distância de South Carolina
11:00 PM - Horário da call Four Seasons
20+ minutos - Duração segunda call Four Seasons
30 minutos - Tempo de espera Four Seasons
$6K - Custo inner circle para Roy + parceiro
3 meses - Tempo sem resultado de Roy
1 semana - Tentativa educação de Roy
4 meses - Tempo decorrido Edward sem ação aparente
1 ano - Pagamento anual de Edward
14 minutos - Vídeo setup Jeremy AI Fase 1
April 12-13 - Próximo mastermind
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Three-Part Framework:
Do-It-Yourself (DIY) - Cursos
Done With You (DWY) - Inner Circle, Consulting, Jeremy AI
Done For You (DFY) - Agency Services
Three Decision Variables:
Price (preço)
Level of Involvement (nível de envolvimento)
Outcome Speed (velocidade de resultado)
Two Primary Upsell Factors:
Money (affordability)
Ease (facilidade/simplicidade)
Three Control Factors:
Who's in control (cliente controla decisão)
Conditions of the deal (condições do negócio)
Timing (momento certo)
Upsell Philosophy:
Want the deal but don't need the deal
Nunca pressionar
Upsell para benefício do cliente, não próprio
Cross-sell baseado no que é melhor para o cliente
Natural Ascension Strategy:
Curso → Inner Circle → Consulting/Agency
House money concept (crescimento paga próximo nível)
Seamless transitions baseadas em resultado
Communication Strategy:
Abrir linha de comunicação
Check-ins regulares de progresso
Pergunta mágica: "Anything I can do to help you more?"
Follow-up do rep que vendeu
Timing Strategies:
Upsell quando cliente está "faminto" (Alinea/Jungsik example)
Não upsell muito cedo (honeymoon phase)
Baseado em outcome achievement ou failure
Quando condições mudam (mais dinheiro, mais equipe, etc.)
Same Product, Different Terms:
Diferentes estruturas de financiamento
Warranty differences
Payment structures
Involvement levels
DIY Support Structure:
Executive assistants
Jeremy AI (com limites para DIY)
Lifetime access ao grupo
Questions melhoram produto
Product Improvement Cycle:
Perguntas expõem gaps no produto
Feedback → novo conteúdo
Weekly group calls adicionam valor perpétuo
Jeremy AI conecta todo conhecimento
Referral/Partnership Strategy:
Amber para operations/dashboards
Sem custo para Jeremy
Expertise em troca de oportunidades de venda naturais
ESTRUTURA:
Pre-Sale Cross-Sell: Determinar oferta certa ANTES de vender
Post-Sale Upsell: Baseado em progresso e resultado
Multi-Brand Showroom Effect: Facilita cross-sell tendo opções side-by-side
Wine Pairing Model: Upsell no momento de maior fome/desejo
Supplement Strategy: Venda quando faminto, antes de consumir produto base
Trade-In Strategy: Reduz out-of-pocket, aumenta affordability
Hybrid Offers: DFY → DWY transition (avançado)
Collective Unit Value: Valor vem do grupo, não só indivíduos
Price Escalation Over Time: OGs pagam menos, novos membros mais
Incentive Alignment: Preço alto o suficiente para dar suporte de qualidade
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Curso DIY: $5,000 one-time
Inner Circle: $4,000-$5,000+/mês (varia por timing de entrada)
Consulting: $20,000/mês
Agency Services: $15,000/mês (exemplo)
Edward Tax Services: $50K-$250K (diferença de $200K por involvement)
ROI Examples:
Andrew: $100K → $200K/mês no inner circle
Cliente agency: $300K → $600K/mês
Garrett White: $22M/ano (consulting → agency)
Upsell Economics:
$11K/mês extra em $300K/mês crescimento = easy decision
House money concept: crescimento paga upsell
Luxury Examples:
McLaren: $105K out-of-pocket de $400K+ total
911: $60K out-of-pocket de $250K+ total
Ferrari 812 GTS: $200K+ out-of-pocket (rejected por money)
Richard Mille: $100K (comprado) vs $300K+ (desejado mas não acessível)
Restaurant Upsells:
Alinea: $295-$795 wine pairing em $375/pessoa base
Jungsik: Supplements em 15-course menu
Rent Examples:
$31,550/mês total (penthouse + segunda casa)
Consideração de custo adicional de venue vs casa melhor
DURAÇÃO: 2h 40min 15s FORMATO: Weekly Inner Circle Group Call, Screen share com slides, Apresentação ao vivo com Q&A, Exemplos pessoais detalhados, Case studies de clientes
